channel.title: Comments for page "Are We There Yet?"
channel.description: Posts in the discussion thread "Are We There Yet?"

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yay! Merle!

item.title: Junior Staff Post
item.content:encoded:
Image Sources: checkpoint.png,railyard.JPG,driving.jpg.

fire.jpg, however, is unsourced, and has thus been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Merle for president.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Merle is Dietrich's name for his Mal0 "buddy".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seriously, who is Merle? There's the link to the Malo SCP, but that isn't helping much. Is it the being in the photograph, some agent involved with the case, or some person's alternate personality?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm really warming up to this little ensemble, though that's carried almost entirely by Alex and Merle.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the "this is just like mtf something-13" part hints at no.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Yeah, where do I download this app again?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Merle really makes this story. Almost everything it says or does is great.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"WHO THE FUCK IS MYRTLE!?!"

Hope you like bullets.

Now that I get WTF is going on in this series, this one's really made me stand up and take notice. :D

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Missed your period.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I was thinking about that. Elmore Leonard used "Rayland" and "Boyd" in "Fire in The Hole" (and therefore in Justified) for a reason. He was deliberately writing a western set in a contemporary setting, so these old fashioned southern names were in support of this. What are the weird names doing here?

Besides, I get to say what I find distracting. You can't really convince me that I don't.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
If memory serves, US TV and film can only use a name if there are less than one or more than three instances of that exact name in the country. So names tend to be either quite common or extremely uncommon.

Fun fact: When the original Star Trek aired, there was a US Navy officer named James Kirk who had just been promoted. Proof that fact can be stranger than fiction…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
fiction can afford no such luxuries.

Frankly speaking, I've watched a fair amount of fictional TV dramas that use weirder and very out-of-place names. I would argue that fiction can be as crazy as it wishes to, so long a story is told.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Real life gets away with being unbelievable because it is undeniably real.

Edit: Or to put it another way, in fiction the author controls every element present. If something doesn't contribute to theme then you should consider not including it, especially if it is a distraction. Weird names make me analyze why they are there. Either I am missing something or these aren't there for a reason. So instead of enjoying the story and thinking about what the author wants me to think about, I am thinking about this. Hence, distracting.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I process medical records for sixteen hours a week (meaning I get to look at dozens of mostly random names each day) and I assure you that people have weirder names than you might think. Granted, it's mostly in the surname department that you find unexpected things, but honestly these names don't strike me as odd at this point.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well we know that Lurk is Dietrich's given name, he says so in one of the other stories. "Django" could be a nickname, but probably should of had quotes then. Foxx was called that even before he became a mole in the Foundation, and MC&D doesn't strike me as a silly names outfit. YMMV.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not unsympathetic, but "the Foundation renames/recruits people with really weird code-names" is such a tradition by now that we couldn't really write Resurrection without it. At the very least, the old characters would have those names.

We could've still made all the new characters be given more ordinary names, but… the general consensus from our writers is that it's more fun this way.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: That's… super… awesome.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dietrich looked at his driver side mirror just in time to a .45 ACP round whiz through it.

Missing a verb

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes I understand that (although "Django" is more properly a nickname). I am saying that Romani usually aren't named "Bridge", "Lurk" had been out of fashion for a long time and that "Aleksandar Foxx" sounds way too much like a comic book character (also what language doesn't have an "x" for "Alexander" but does for "Fox"?). One person with an unlikely name is maybe cool, but not everyone…

Also while real life can get away with not bothering to be realistic, fiction can afford no such luxuries. Again though, this appears to be just my problem, so no worries!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'm not saying that Django Bridge or Lurk Dietrich are actual names in the real world. I'm saying that "Django" and "Bridge" are valid names, as are "Lurk" and "Dietrich".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes that's what I mean, everytime I remember that these characters have these names… I guess it is just me. Carry on.

Edit: I seriously doubt there are many Django Bridges in this world. It is very jarring to have so many unlikely named people in the same story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Those are actual first and last names that have been used before, unless you think the names themselves are too silly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I really want to like these stories, but every time I remember that Dietrich is named "Lurk" bridge is "Django" and Aleksander Fox is still named… that, it takes me right out of it. :(

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Props to the artist; that's the correct way to sign "kill" in ASL.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Jeez, and I was just getting used to Merle being a harmless silent snarker. I wonder if this is the first time he's told Dietrich to kill someone who was close to figuring them out.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Since I found it on the Most Recently Created page, At first I thought it was an AWCY? tale.

Then I clicked the link.

Then I upvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Hey, got some fanart over this tale!

http://maraquanwocky.tumblr.com/post/119490999135/he-will-report-us-kill-him-before-they-arrive
